summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Question of whether the father poses an unacceptable risk of harm to the child – Where in 2019 the father pleaded guilty to criminal offences and received a wholly suspended sentence for three years – Where the father seeks unsupervised time with the child – No orders made for the child to spend time or communicate with the father

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

INTRODUCTION

These proceedings relate to the Applicant father Mr Parry (“the father”) aged 45 years old and the Respondent mother Ms Davron (“the mother”) aged 36 years old and their daughter, X who is now nine years of age (“the child”). At the core of these proceedings arises an assessment of risk and whether the father should spend any time with his child and if the mother can facilitate any such time.

CONTEXTUAL HISTORY

The parents Mr Parry and Ms Davron commenced their relationship some time in 2008 resulting in them cohabitating in early 2010 and marrying in 2014.
In 2016, the parties welcomed the birth of their only child, X.
In 2018, the father was charged with two counts of possessing child exploitation material (“CEM”) and one count of distributing child exploitation material. Such charges are discussed more fulsomely later in these Reasons.
As is not uncommon when a parent is charged with such a serious offence, the Department of Children, Youth Justice and Multicultural Affairs (as it then was) (“the Department”) attended the parties’ home in September 2018. That visit resulted in the parties agreeing to a safety plan which provided for the father to leave the matrimonial home and for the child to have no unsupervised contact with her father, with such arrangement to be reviewed a week thereafter.
Between the months of September and early October 2018, the mother says the child spent some time with the father supervised by the paternal grandmother – but in the mother’s home.
In late 2018, the father signed a bail undertaking which included conditions that he shall not spend time with the child.
A month later, the father’s bail conditions were varied, resulting in time between the father and the child to recommence and occur each Sunday from 9.00am until 11.00am in the paternal grandmother’s presence.
At paragraph 165 of her trial affidavit, the mother deposes to commencing a relationship with Mr B in “late 2019”.
In late 2019, the father was sentenced (see Annexure 2 of the father’s affidavit filed 14 February 2022) in respect of multiple charges, resulting in the District Court ordered on each charge a term of imprisonment.
On 15 February 2021, the father commenced proceedings in Division 2 of this Court, where the matter progressed through the usual case management pathway.
Most notably, on 7 May 2021, a Senior Judicial Registrar made Orders on an interim basis that:
1. [X] born [2016] (the child) live with the mother.

2. The mother have sole parental responsibility for the child.

3. The child have no time or communication with the father.
The father be restrained and an injunction issue restraining the father from contacting or approaching, or attempting to contact or approach the child.
On such occasion a suite of orders were made for the father to attend upon reportable counselling and for a sexual risk assessment report to be prepared at the father’s expense. The matter was also listed for a final hearing of three days commencing on 14 March 2022. In her Reasons for Judgment, the Registrar noted that the parties had secured an expediated trial listing.
The mother deposes in her trial affidavit to marrying Mr B in 2021 and giving birth to their first child C in 2021. At the time of the hearing, the mother’s household consisted of herself and X; Mr B and his two children from a previous relationship, being E and D; and C, the biological son of the mother and Mr B. Further, Ms Oakley, Counsel appearing on behalf of the mother, informed the Court from the bar table that the mother was pregnant.
On 4 March 2022, that same Senior Judicial Registrar vacated those trial dates upon the oral application of the father, and a new trial was listed to commence on 16 November 2022.
On 23 May 2022, a Judge of Division 2 transferred the proceedings to Division 1, with the matter being listed before me on 10 June 2022 for case management hearing. On that occasion, I set the matter down for a final hearing before me commencing on 12 December 2022.
The final hearing progressed before me for three days and was adjourned to 20 February 2023 for oral submissions. On that occasion judgment was reserved to a date to be advised. The Court expresses to the parties its regret in not publishing these Reasons more quickly, as they reasonably could have expected.
On 27 June 2025, the matter was re-listed for case management hearing to provide the parties with an opportunity to seek to adduce further evidence in relation to the proceedings. Neither party sought to produce any further updating evidence to the Court, the Court being told that nothing of significance had happened since the trial finished in December 2022.

STATUTORY PATHWAY

In all cases involving parenting orders, the child’s best interests are the Court’s paramount consideration. In determining those interests the Court must consider not only the objects of s 60B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and the right of a child to have a meaningful relationship with all those people significant to them, but also the primary considerations under s 60CC(2) and the additional considerations under s 60CC(3) which will be analysed below to ensure that the order I propose will serve the best interests of the child.
To the extent possible, the Court should ensure orders made do not expose a party or a child to unacceptable risk of harm through family violence, abuse or neglect.
In certain circumstances the Court applies a statutory presumption that it is in the child’s best interests for parents to have equal shared parental responsibility (s 61DA(1)), which relates to making major decisions and not about the time a child spends with each parent.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

The father

Counsel for the father, Mr George, delivered both oral and written submissions on 20 February 2023, with the father’s proposed orders (which are annexed to these Reasons and marked as Appendix One) essentially seeking orders that:
(a) X live with the mother and that she have sole parental responsibility;

(b) X commence spending time with the father and graduating as follows:

(i) For four months, supervised at a contact centre for two to three hours, every alternating Saturday/Sunday;

(ii) Then for a further four months, three hours every alternating Saturday or Sunday at the home of and supervised by either the paternal grandparents or paternal uncle;

(iii) Then for a further four months, unsupervised time for five hours each alternating Saturday and Sunday;

(iv) Then for four months, each alternate weekend from 10.00am Saturday to 4.00pm Sunday (overnight);

(v) Then for a further four months, each alternate weekend from after school Friday to before school Monday, telephone time on three occasions a week and half of school holidays and special days; and

(vi) From when X turns 12 years of age (which will be 2028), week about time with changeovers on the Friday.

A fairly standard suite of specific issues orders is proposed. It is clear that the carefully crafted orders seek to increase time with supervision over the first eight months before unsupervised time commences, with overnight time commencing after 12 months.

The mother

Counsel for the mother, Ms Oakley, maintained in her oral submissions the mother’s position, articulated in her case outline filed 7 December 2022, that the child live with the mother; that she have sole parental responsibility and that “there be no orders for the child to spend time with or communicate with the father”. Whilst I shall deal with more fully the submissions made, in essence it is contended on behalf of the mother that the father is an unacceptable risk to the child; the risk cannot be ameliorated by time being supervised by the paternal family and it is not in the best interests of X to begin time now, when it could not progress further in the future.

Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”)

Counsel for the ICL, Mr Bunning, submitted that the orders sought by the ICL and set out in the case outline filed 30 November 2022, namely for the child to spend no time with the father are the orders which are in the best interests of X on all the evidence.
Clearly therefore, the Court is asked to determine if the father presents as a risk to X and if so, can the risk be ameliorated such that the child’s right to spend time with the father can occur safely into the future. As this is the seminal issue for determination, I deal next in these Reasons with the issue of risk. In so doing, I am mindful of the Full Court decision of Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092, where the Court considered and analysed a number of earlier decisions before adopting the analysis of Austin J in Fitzwater & Fitzwater [2019] FamCAFC 251; (2019) 60 Fam LR 212 (particularly at [132] to [142]) as being a correct statement of the law, namely in summary:
(a) the assessment of risk is a predictive exercise, liable to be influenced by factual findings about past events. The contemplation of risk entails the foresight of possible harm. It is an oddity to expect that the mere possibility of future harm can or should be proven as a probability (at [138] of Fitzwater (supra));

(b) the higher the chance of the children’s sexual abuse, the greater the risk of their physical or psychological harm. At some point on the continuum, the risk of such harm becomes so potent it cannot be tolerated: it is unacceptable (at [139] of Fitzwater);

(c) it cannot be correct that the unacceptable risk of a child’s sufferance of harm through future sexual abuse can only be established if it is proven as a fact on the balance of probabilities, that the child (or another) had already been sexually abused in the past (at [140] of Fitzwater); and

(d) in child related proceedings, a comparatively small risk of really serious harm can justify action, while even the virtual certainty of slight harm might not. It could hardly be otherwise, because no prudent adult would willingly expose a child to the risk of sexual abuse when there is an unacceptable high chance of its occurrence, even though the chance is not proven by the evidence to be probable. Requiring the proof of any possible future child abuse as a probability would pervert the law as settled by the High Court in M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69 and Malec v J C Hutton Proprietary Limited [1990] HCA 20; (1990) 169 CLR 638 (per [142] of Fitzwater).

More recently since Isles & Nelissen (supra) was decided, later Full Court authority has succinctly summarised Isles & Nelissen – for example, in Pacotto & Galvin [2025] FedCFamC1A 27 at [38], namely:
As for risk, or unacceptable risk, which is the heart of this appeal, the Full Court clarified in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 (“Isles & Nelissen”) that past allegations ... are to be determined by reference to s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) and that the assessment of an unacceptable risk cannot be measured by the civil standard of proof; rather, the court is required to look at possibilities. The factual findings made about past events inform the task of the risk assessment predicting the safety of the child or the parent...

MATERIAL RELIED UPON BY THE PARTIES

The father

As detailed in his case outline filed 5 December 2022, the following witnesses were relied upon by the father:
(a) Himself, by affidavit filed 10 November 2022;

(b) Solicitor Mr F filed 10 November 2022 relating to events in July 2018 when the mother alleged she was “pressured” into signing an affidavit on 1 August 2018 requesting that the requirement for the father to be supervised around X be removed;

(c) Two affidavits of Clinical Psychologist Dr G filed 29 July 2021 and 14 November 2022;

(d) Affidavit of the paternal grandmother Mrs H filed 14 February 2022;

(e) Affidavit of the paternal grandfather Mr J filed 14 February 2022;

(f) Affidavit of the father’s sister-in-law Ms K filed 14 February 2022;

(g) Affidavit of the father’s brother Mr L filed 14 February 2022;

(h) Affidavit of the father’s brother Mr M filed 14 February 2022; and

(i) Affidavit of Clinical and Forensic Psychologist Mr N filed 16 September 2021.

Mr F was not required for cross-examination, but all other witnesses were the subject of cross-examination. I was persuaded that the father’s cross-examination should take place after all his witnesses had given their evidence.

The mother

As detailed in her case outline filed 7 December 2022, the mother was the only witness in her case, relying upon short affidavits filed 23 February 2022 and 14 November 2022. In circumstances where it was accepted that X would remain living in the mother’s home, no issue arose for exploration as to any relevant evidence from the mother’s husband Mr B, who the mother married in 2021 after commencing a relationship with the mother in late 2019. I was not asked accordingly to make any adverse inference as to his lack of evidence, and no basis to do so exists.
The ICL, as detailed in her case outline filed 30 November 2022, relied primarily upon the affidavit of independent Psychiatrist Dr O filed 4 November 2021 and the family report prepared by Child Court Expert Ms Q dated 6 December 2021 – both of whom were the subject of cross-examination. A child dispute conference memorandum by Family Consultant Ms P dated 20 April 2021 was relied upon for historical context.
For completeness, a number of documents were tendered as follows:
Exhibit 1
	
-
	
Document used by Mr N – Abel and Becker Cognition Scale

Exhibit 2
	
-
	
Hansen Sex Attitudes questionnaire

Exhibit 3
	
-
	
Page signed by the father about child exploitation material

Exhibit 4
	
-
	
Pre-sentencing report of Dr G

Exhibit 5
	
-
	
Correspondence from Dr G to Drs T and S dated 18 September 2018 and to Dr U dated 4 April 2019

Exhibit 6
	
-
	
Internet search history from the brief of the Director of Public Prosecutions

Exhibit 7
	
-
	
Letter from the ICL to Dr O dated 6 December 2022, providing updating material

Exhibit 8
	
-
	
Family report and letter from the ICL to the report writer dated 6 December 2022 providing updating material

Exhibit 9
	
-
	
The ICL’s tender bundle containing:

	

	
- documents produced by the Department

	

	
- documents produced by Ms V, W Mental Health Services

	

	
- documents produced by the Director of Public Prosecutions

	

	
- documents produced by the District Court of Queensland

	

	
- notice of reportable offenders reporting obligations

	

	
- quarterly reports completed by the father in accordance with his reporting obligations

EVIDENCE RELATING TO RISK

In no particular order of priority, I deal now with various aspects of the evidence adduced as it touches on the issue of risk.

The criminal charges

The father says, and I accept, that during a period when the family business failed causing him to establish his own business (maintaining employment for a number of staff), the father started accessing CEM and adult pornography on the internet. I accept the father predominantly accessed these sites whilst at work and that the mother was totally unaware of the father’s internet activity.
In the father’s written submissions at paragraph 133, his Counsel described the father’s actions in the past, in accessing CEM as “deplorable, abhorrent and shameful”. I agree.
The father was arrested and charged in 2018 with multiple counts, being:
(a) between 2016 and 2018, he possessed child exploitation material; and

(b) in 2018 he distributed child exploitation material.

A Statement of Facts (“Annexure [MRP1]”) was created by the Director of Public Prosecutions (“DPP”) to assist the sentencing judge. I see no reason now, to further seek to identify whether some aspects of that Statement of Facts might be inaccurate or could have been expressed differently. There is no need to record some of the images depicted by police in the four hard drives located by police in the main office, save to note that the Statement of Facts confirmed the number of images being over 1900. The father, at the time of arrest admitted to police he downloaded images of female children aged from 11 years, and adult pornography at work because he was “getting bored” and did so when other people were not around because it was his “dirty little secret”. He claimed that he did not become sexually aroused by the CEM but looked at it because they were nice pictures of “pretty young girls”. Police officer statements were tendered (part of Exhibit 9). Annexure 2 and Annexure 3 to the affidavit of the father sworn 14 February 2022 are the sentencing remarks of the District Court Judge of late 2019 and a pre-sentence Psychologist’s report of Clinical Psychologist Dr Y.
The sentencing judge, when considering the possession charge, indicated that all but a few of the images were classed as category one images and movies depicting prepubescent girls. When considering the distribution charge, the sentencing judge referred to one “accessible zip folder containing over 2600 images – of prepubescent girls in various stages of undress, and engaging in penetrative and vaginal sex with adult men”.
The sentencing judge referred to Dr Y’s report, where she opined the father was a low risk of reoffending, however he gave limited weight to the report as it was based only on a two hour interview. The sentencing judge found more helpful, the report from the father’s treating Psychologist Dr G, who has opined the father was very remorseful for accessing the material and considered that the father’s “collection of pornographic material is an extreme and dysfunctional example of his maladaptive coping strategies at the time”. Dr G’s full report is before the Court as Exhibit 4. For context, the father’s offending pursuant to charge one commenced when X was a toddler.

Treating Psychologist Dr G

Before turning to the reports prepared by Dr G, a Clinical Psychologist, it was relevant to record that in the cross-examination, Dr G said, inter alia:
(a) it is outside his area of expertise to assess the father’s prospect of reoffending, and whilst in his report dated 6 July 2021 he discussed the risk of reoffending, he acknowledged at that time to the patient the limitations of his clinical expertise with respect to criminal and forensic settings, and he encouraged the father to engage with a Forensic Psychiatrist or Psychologist; and

(b) his therapeutic focus was initially to assist the father with his anxiety and depression leading up to the day of sentence, and ultimately, part of his role was to assist the father to understand why he was interested in searching that type of material.

In this context, the evidence in his reports, I summarise as follows:
(a) The report dated 6 July 2021 was prepared after the father had attended 19 sessions, and the therapy was:
(i) directed to supporting the father “to maintain a balance in his life and prioritising areas that bring purpose, meaning, and fulfilment”; and

(ii) furthermore, to enhance his awareness and better understand how his offending behaviour developed, the seriousness of his offending, and the impacts of his offending on others. Whilst Dr G was “struck by the determination and importance [the father] has placed on maintaining and re-establishing a relationship with [X]”, he recorded that the father says he has expressed being subject to “perceived bias and prejudice” as well as holding fears information will be distorted from those “officials” he has dealt with through the process of seeking to establish a relationship with X. Again, in this report, Dr G highlights the limits of his clinical experience and the likely importance “of objectively demonstrating and reassuring others as to his safety”. Dr G indicated why the father would benefit from the treatment/assessment of a specialist, with forensic expertise that may help him demonstrate safety.

(b) Exhibit 4 indicated the father originally presented in August 2018 and had been attending fortnightly (a total of 30 sessions). The report provides a self-reported context, suggesting where “the collecting and search for material was made more important than his gratification and interest in the material” and that during treatment “he has continued to maintain a strong sense of guilt and shame in connection with being discovered with the material”. Dr G’s assessment at this time was that the father had engaged well in psychological treatment. The report tries to attribute, in my assessment, his past maladaptive behaviour as being due to a “joyless” existence and to focus on the needs of others “while ignoring his own needs”. Frankly, the tenor of the report seemed to demonstrate a lack of insight by the father;

(c) The most recent report dated 22 May 2022 was mostly repetitive, in my assessment, save for what Dr G described as “perhaps the greatest breakthrough” which resulted “from attempts to better understand the origins of [Mr Parry’s] apparent fixation with very specific subject matter of the child exploitation material he was drawn to, namely [...].” Again, it is suggested – I infer as some sort of excuse – that the period after the birth of X the home environment caused the father to feel “trapped in his miserable and dreary existence”. Dr G opines that the father’s “light bulb moment” caused the father to experience a “strange sense of relief” that enabled him to recognise:

the possibility of his behaviour as more of a misguided attempt to connect with a happier (happiest) time in his life. This is in contrast to his previous reaction, that being deeply troubled/confused, ashamed and revolted when he couldn’t understand why his downloading of this material, particularly if this material, particularly if this instead pointed to a more paraphilic basis for this interest.
Importantly, and it appears to me for the first time in his reports, Dr G says “while he has never denied the initial use of pornography and his sexual attraction to this material in the first place” the amassing of vast quantities of files was a result of “his compulsive and obsessive focus” on the searching, organisation and collection to complete sets for different “models”. Dr G referred to receiving reports from the Court proceedings and adopted Mr N’s assessment that the father “does not represent any physical or emotional risk to [children] generally, or his daughter specifically”. Overall, the opinion of Dr G is for a positive prognosis.
As would be anticipated, Dr G’s evidence was strenuously explored by Counsel for the mother and the ICL, from which some further relevant evidence flowed, including:
(a) pre-pubescent girls did not seem to be his interest, and he has never said he liked looking at 11 year old girls;

(b) the father used CEM to “numb” or “cope” with some of his psychological pain;

(c) Dr G, seemingly a little naïve about how Courts operate, said he assumed if the father had an “identified risk”, he would be referred by Dr O and/or Mr N to an expert for further therapy;

(d) the father initially, by referring to the girls as “models”, considered they were not victims, but through his therapy he ultimately accepted they were victims and initially he was misguided;

(e) the father told him he had “deleted” some material that “disgusted” him and catalogued the other material;

(f) he could not recall when, during therapy, he first became aware of his lack of empathy towards the mother and the child; and

(g) whilst his reports to the father’s other health professionals (see Exhibit 5) referred to the father obtaining additional support (in the form of a Forensic Psychologist), he cannot recall now whether the Psychologist he was thinking of was available.

In the conclusions/findings on this discrete topic, I will refer back to some of the evidence of Dr G.

Mr N

Mr N, a Clinical and Forensic Psychologist, was engaged pursuant to an Order of the Court on 7 May 2021 to provide the father with reportable counselling to:
(a) provide psychoeducation regarding the impact of child sexual abuse and exploitation on victims, families and communities;

(b) facilitate the father’s ability to accept responsibility for his offending behaviours; and

(c) develop a safety plan to reduce the possibility of future reoffending,

and to prepare a report on the progress made by the father during counselling.

His report dated 9 September 2021 expresses opinions within the parameters of his remit and confirms the father commenced attending upon him in June 2021 and had five further sessions, concluding in August 2021, and the report sets out in some detail the process of assessment, with the primary focus being an assessment of risk.
In concluding at paragraph 125, that the father does not represent any physical or emotional risk to children generally or his daughter specifically, Mr N founded his conclusion on the following assessments by him, namely:
(a) the father never reported or referred to his offending in any manner other than accepting full responsibility for his actions;

(b) his responses to therapeutic challenges, directions and homework exercises reflected reasonable commitment to treatment. He holds some confidence he will continue to attend upon his Psychologist;

(c) in consideration of all assessments conducted to date, and notwithstanding his previous involvement and convictions for possession and distributing CEM, Mr N could detect none of the critical, psycho-sexual areas of assessment and treatment interest, including particularly paraphilias, limited capacity for empathy, or poor impulse control behaviours as part of the father’s current functioning;

(d) Mr N could not detect any current tendency towards unusual secrecy and/or obsessional independence, significantly poor social support levels, or networking with other persons having child (sexual) interests of concern;

(e) critically, assessment of the father did not detect cognitive distortions on his part; and

(f) the father’s responses and discussions designed to extract and identify his current attitudes and values in respect of CEM, as summarised at paragraph 124, do not raise issues of concern.

Again, as would be expected, Mr N was cross-examined by Counsel for the mother and the ICL, from which some further relevant evidence was given, including:
(a) to get context and gauge the level of family support, he also spoke to the father’s father;

(b) if the father had given him an explanation for his offending, it would be in his report. The father was ambivalent about the charges, but did not try to justify his behaviour;

(c) the father’s comments indicate the pathway to the opportunity to offend was his “social isolation”;

(d) the father did not say he was aroused by the sexual material or that he used his viewing for sexual gratification;

(e) during his consultations, the issue of “[his interest in very specific subject matter ]” did not arise. If it had he would likely have explored whether this was a fetish;

(f) all psychometric testing was based on self-reporting by the father. The father’s scores raised no concerns;

(g) he received little by way of “collateral material” and does not recall receiving the report of Psychologist Dr Y;

(h) he “never” says a patient assessed is “no” risk, but he maintained his assessment the father was a “low” risk and not a “moderate” or “high risk”;

(i) the sessions with him ceased when he knew the father was maintaining therapy with Dr G; and

(j) the grading of the images is, in his view, relevant to nature of the offending.

As I further discuss below, this evidence is part of the overall assessment of risk.

DR O

Dr O, an independent consultant Psychiatrist was retained by the ICL to prepare a report as to the functioning of the father generally and a sexual risk assessment. He not only provides in his report dated 21 October 2021 (arising from an assessment of the father made during interviews earlier in October 2021), details of the sources of information, and as is his style, a full transcript of the interview with the father is attached. During Dr O’s cross-examination, it was not suggested to him that on the basis of the transcript he misunderstood the father’s statements.
When dealing with functioning, Dr O opined there was no support now for a diagnosis of an active psychiatric illness or mental disorder which could result in an uncreased risk of parenting dysfunction. The areas of potential concern (at page 8, paragraphs 35 to 40), were now assessed as low “given the lifestyle changes that [Mr Parry] has initiated and the therapy he has undertaken”. Dr O says the father needs “ongoing specific supportive counselling using his report as a template”, so as to work on a long term plan to keep control of his internet use, to learn from his history of personal relationships, and to work towards a new, more amicable phase of post separation parenting”. Dr O reasoned that the mental health professional with the training and expertise to best assess and manage these complex issues is a medical specialist consultant Psychiatrist.
After recording a number of positive aspects of the father’s history and presentation (page 10, paragraph 25 to page 11 paragraph 35), Dr O opined that:
(a) therefore, the only concern is terms of the potential risk for the future in terms of potential offending behaviour which requires risk management, and which must be ameliorated with ongoing psychological management is:
His insight into his offending behaviour and the cause issues of concern. This is because the interview with [Mr Parry] revealed he remained perplexed as to the origins of his offending behaviour.

This is particularly important as [Mr Parry] clearly articulated a history at interview in which he had no insight into the odd and indeed bizarre use of the internet at his workplace at [Town Z], which he repeated on many occasions, over a long period of time, and which become so obsessive that he feared police might attend the workplace, yet he did not seek medical assessment or aide, and continued to not be in control of internet use.

(As per the original)
The Hare Psychopathy Checklist SVR20 and HCR20 was assessed as low risk.
Again, as would be expected, the ICL’s expert witness was the subject of cross-examination by Counsel for the father and Counsel for the mother from which some further relevant evidence included that:
(a) he had not read the report of Mr N but his assessment of the father being a low risk of harm, although congruent with the assessment by Mr N did not erase completely the concerns Dr O formed about the father’s depth of understanding of his behaviour, and on all the data he remained of the view that there is a “degree of superficiality” in the father;

(b) he remained concerned about “the capacity of the father to be truly changed” and his ability to be aware of his “weaknesses” and he drew comfort from the report of Dr G as to ongoing therapy, and the steps the father has taken;

(c) he is not aware of any studies which support a link between accessing CEM with child sexual abuse and the assessment and interviews did not suggest this was the father’s orientation; and

(d) when directed to the report or Dr G, Dr O could not recall the father advising him of the “light bulb moment”. Whilst Dr G had himself identified it is likely to be helpful for the father to engage with a specialist forensic Psychologist for treatment – likely to be 12-month program. He provided the Court with the name of a recommended practitioner in the Brisbane rea.

FAMILY REPORT WRITER – MS Q

Court Child Expert/Family Consultant, Ms Q, published a report dated 6 December 2021, as ordered by a Senior Judicial Registrar in the case management pathway. Although as is usual within a family report process to concentrate on the dynamics of the parents’ relationship and the consideration of children’s wishes and needs, a focus on risks is also standard procedure – often mental health, drug use, family violence and alleged sexual abuse.
So far as this case is concerned, where there is no evidence of any sexual abuse of the child or any other child by the father (or allegations of such), the area of risk is how the father’s accessing CEM and/or adult pornography and his response to being charged and convicted (on his guilty plea) for such criminal behaviour can be assessed as posing a risk to X. Later in these Reasons, it will of course be necessary to consider the evidence as to what orders are in the best interests of the child – but the cases advanced by the experienced Counsel almost invite a finding as to risk as an initial question. I accept that whatever finding I make needs to be absorbed into the more holistic consideration of other factors prescribed by the Act, when seeking to determine what orders are in the best interests of X.
With this preamble, it is obvious that in making her recommendation at paragraph 112 that X spend no time with her father, and further at paragraph 113, that if the Court decides that X should spend time with the father, then this time be supervised, Ms Q considered and assessed a number of factors, including:
(a) the records of child protection services and police services (paragraph 8);

(b) the report of Dr O and records provided by Dr G;

(c) the father’s version of the history, including his offending (paragraphs 33 to 42);

(d) the mother’s version of the history (paragraphs 43 to 45), before the report writer collected further information under the heading of “child safety and well-being” at paragraphs 45 to 59.

(e) the father’s mental health issues where Ms Q considered the reports of Dr G and Dr O (paragraphs 63 to 70);

(f) within her evaluation at paragraphs 84 to 100, Ms Q identified the main issue in dispute is if the father poses a risk towards X and if there is a benefit for X in re-establishing a relationship with her father, during which evaluation Ms Q’s opinions include:

(i) at paragraph 87, she says she had information from the mother which “could indicate a significant risk in terms of [Mr Parry’s] risk of further offending”;

(ii) at paragraph 89, she says some of the past sexual behaviour of the father would be considered “deviant” sexualised behaviours when undertaking a risk assessment and the sexual activities in the parents’ sexual relationship “when in the context of a convicted sex offender are of concern”;

(iii) at paragraph 92, she opines the father “showed limited insight into how his offending really impacted on the mother and [X]” and he showed “limited victim empathy”;

(iv) at paragraph 94, Ms Q seemed to draw a possible connection between convictions of a “non contact” nature escalating to offences of directly harming children – before acknowledging she cannot predict if the father’s behaviour would escalate;

(v) at paragraph 96, in circumstances where the father denies that he had any sexual gratification when he committed these offences, this means “that there is still uncertainty about the nature of why he committed these offences”. Ms Q said that she was not convinced the nature and duration of the offences “correlate to [Mr Parry’s] explanations”;

(vi) finally, at paragraphs 109 and 110, Ms Q says:

This matter is complex, however, the main issues are if the father poses a risk to [X]. When determining this risk, I think that a decision has to be made by the Court on whether the father’s offending behaviour was motivated by sexual gratification or if his behaviour was somewhat a consequence of maladaptive coping mechanisms that presented with impulsive and obsessive behaviours with no sexual pleasure or desire for the father.
My view is that the father lacks insight into his offending behaviour. I cannot say that this means he is at risk of sexually harmful behaviours towards [X] in the future. However, I am concerned that the risks are unknown due to the father’s minimisation of his actions and behaviours. Furthermore, in my view, he has not fully considered [X’s] best interests and he is prioritising his needs and his rights over that of [X].
Counsel for the mother and the father explore the opinions of Ms Q in cross-examination, from which some further evidence was given, including:
(a) she has now read Mr N’s report but it did not change her perspective, as she still sees an “ongoing risk”;

(b) Ms Q rejected the proposition put to her by Counsel for the father that determining risks associated with the father’s sexual preferences was beyond her expertise;

(c) she asserted that, in her view, there is a connection between CEM and sexual abuse of young children;

(d) her concerns were partly based on the “deviant” sexual behaviour at an adult level (including dressing up and videoing sex);

(e) she had read the Department notes and their assessed concern about the potential for sexual abuse;

(f) she did not “disregard” the sexual risk assessments by other persons but felt less weight could be given for entirely self-reported behaviour without their being context for the mother about the adult sexual relationship; and

(g) by reference to paragraph 104 of her report, she holds the view that “every adult” could be cautious and suspicious of the father and X could become aware of this.

Finally, despite quite focussed cross-examination by Mr George for the father (all within appropriate limits), she said her assessment was not “wrong”; the father demonstrates limited insight and, in her view, takes no responsibility for the effect of his offending.

OTHER FAMILY WITNESSES

The father’s family shared their support for him, by not only signing affidavits but being available for cross-examination.
There was one common theme from all their evidence at least and that was that they had no idea of the father’s offending and were shocked when he was charged; they had generally no knowledge of the nature of the images; they all held a firm view that he presents no risk at all to X.
My observation was that many of the family were in Court for some of the testimony of the experts, who followed their evidence. As they generally had not read any of the expert evidence, they had no context, in my view, about the complexities.
The family love the father; see him as no risk and have observed the father’s pain in not having time with X. Of course, this also means none of them as grandparents, uncles and aunts, have had a chance to spend time with X, and in the case of Ms K and Mr L’s children and Mr M’s children, no chance to let the cousins develop a relationship. It is not lost on me that the orders proposed by the mother and the ICL (for no time) would prolong and compound the sense of loss and grief the father and his family feel at this time.

THE SUBMISSIONS RECEIVED AS TO RISK

Although I accept the father bears no onus to establish he is not a risk, in the context of the unified position adopted by the mother and the ICL, I choose to examine the very detailed and considered written submissions of the father’s Counsel, Mr George, filed 14 February 2023. I can observe there is nothing more that Mr George could have said or written to support the contention of the father that as there is no “evidence” to support a finding that the father:
(a) has sexually abused any child;

(b) has sexually abused X;

(c) by accessing CEM, poses a greater risk of sexually abusing any person (let alone his own child); nor

(d) presents a real possibility of sexually abusing X in the future,

such that it is in X’s best interests that orders be made that enable a gradual reintroduction of her father (and his family) into X’s life – even to the extent that time supervised by his family (if considered appropriate) could be ordered, “not because he accepts any proper basis for the same, but to endeavour to assuage the mother’s alleged concerns” (see generally submissions at paragraphs 128 to 149 of the father’s written submissions).

In reaching this conclusion, the father relies upon the following contentions, inter alia:
(a) Relying on the mother’s Notice of Risk filed 19 March 2021, no risks were then identified – but the mother now says the child is at risk of sexual abuse by the father;

(b) The mother asserts the father has absolutely nothing to offer the child and holds no concerns for the child of having no future relationship with the father or his extended family;

(c) Despite being aware of the facts surrounding the charges laid against the father, the mother permitted the father to remain in the family home for two months (until Department intervention), and permitted X, between September 2018 and October 2019, to spend supervised time with the father every Sunday for two hours. Furthermore, the mother, in August 2018, signed an affidavit supporting the father having unsupervised time with X. The mother’s actions are at that time totally inconsistent with her holding a genuine belief that the father was a risk to X. The father asserts, at paragraph 37, that the mother “knew the man and she knew (at the onset) that [X] was not a risk of harm in his care.” It is contended that it was only after intervention by the Department that the mother’s concerns were heightened;

(d) The father says that despite the mother’s changing beliefs, they are not supported by the independent experts;

(e) At paragraphs 47 to 61 of the father’s submissions, careful analysis of the Department’s involvement from July 2018 is undertaken, from which the father contends that:

(i) the initial conclusion by the Department, based solely on the charges laid against the father, that X was at an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse, was not the result of any real investigation and “has regrettably shaped the history of this matter”;

(ii) the subsequent further assessment in September 2018 of continuing risk of potential sexual abuse, appears to be “the personal opinion of the case workers – unsupported and not corroborated in any way by actual evidence”;

(iii) this earlier conclusion was reinforced by a further opinion expressed in September 2018 based, it seems, on information from “an unidentified professional notifier” about the content found on the father’s devices;

(iv) the Department records a conclusion about the father’s veracity, and by April 2020 and after expressing that “the mother is acting extremely protectively and is not allowing any access to the child at present”, the Department says (reinforcing the mother’s beliefs) that “[s]hould the father have unsupervised access to the child in the future the child will be put at risk of harm and a child protection notification is warranted”; and

(v) the father asserts that the assumption reached by the Department since 2018 “grew in strength by repetition over the ensuing years” – and that “all judicial officers previously involved in this case, as well as the author of the s.11F [r]eport and the [f]amily [r]eport writer (Ms [Q]), have made the same erroneous assumption”. No such “presumption of risk” arising from the nature of the charges laid against the father can validly exist.

(f) At paragraphs 62 to 68 of the submissions, Counsel summarises the father’s case, submitting that the father has shown insight and remorse and, through counselling from Dr G, which is ongoing, he is supported in his determined endeavour to “prove his trust worthiness and [X’s] safety around him”;

(g) It is submitted (at paragraphs 85 to 92), that the Court Child Expert Ms Q was not truly impartial and was “overly influenced by her assumptions and by the concerns and information provided to her by the mother”. Much of her report “contains commentary and opinions beyond...her area of expertise as a Social Worker” and as a result the Court should “afford the [r]eport minimal weight”;

(h) The Court should accept the conclusions and opinions expressed by Dr O which were not seriously challenged; and

(i) The evidence of Mr N, as summarised in the submissions at paragraphs 99 to 112, should be accepted – particularly the conclusion reached and articulated at paragraph 125 of his report – that the father does not represent any physical or emotional risk to children generally or his daughter specifically.

The submissions of the ICL and on behalf of the mother were shaped by the benefit of reading the considered submissions of the father, previously referred to in these Reasons, but their conjoint position that there should be no orders for X to spend time with the father, and were primarily based (but not solely based) on their contention the father presents as an unacceptable risk to X. Without feeling a need to identify the particular submissions of either the ICL (delivered by Mr Bunning) or the mother (delivered by Ms Oakley), it was apparent they were on the same page, by asserting inter alia that:
(a) the Court should make a finding that the father is an unacceptable risk to X;

(b) the Court should remain concerned that, as is apparent the father had at least amassed 2619 images – but the police also say there were further “files” not accessible to police. Dr G was told some images involved children having sex with adults, but the father disputed any sexual interest in pre-pubescent girls;

(c) the Court must still be concerned by the father’s inability to admit and identify the reasons for his motivation to access the CEM, and his evidence about “collecting folders of child models”, which included children younger than 11 years of age. Dr G expressed a difficulty in accepting the father’s assertion that none of his behaviour was for “sexual gratification” but as already noted, Dr G did not regard himself as an expert in such issues;

(d) although Dr O opined that he was not aware of any evidence to support a connection between a person accessing CEM and then sexually abusing a child, Dr O’s assessment was the father could not be regarded as a person with no risk of reoffending. In particularly, Dr O’s strong view that the father should engage a Psychiatrist was not taken up by the father. Even Dr G recommendation for the father to engage with a forensic psychologist with expertise in this area was not taken up by the father;

(e) although Ms Q was of the view that there is a link between accessing CEM and sexual abuse, she maintained her view that on all the data available to her, the father presents as a risk and only contact via cards, letters and gifts would be in X’s best interests;

(f) the mother’s Counsel particularly contended that the father’s versions of why he was tempted to “collect images of child models” was “inherently untrue” and he was an evasive witness; prone to seek refuge in statements like “I cannot recall”, and deflected now to asserting his extensive therapy with Dr G has created greater insight into his behaviour;

(g) the therapy with Dr G has served little purpose, particularly where Dr G himself acknowledged no expertise in the area. The Court should be concerned that the father has failed to engage with an expert forensic psychologist as has been recommended by Dr Y, Dr G and also Dr O;

(h) the father has failed to fundamentally engage in the question – why was he attracted (and seemingly became somewhat obsessed) with collecting this material?

(i) the paternal family has no real understanding of the reasons for the father’s offending and were shocked when he was charged; and

(j) the father’s attempts to try and justify his actions on the unhappiness in his relationship with the mother at the time (noting the father’s conduct in collecting CEM was not one isolated endeavour, but that it proceeded over some time), was a weak excuse which did the father no credit. Similarly, although clearly he was under significant pressure at work (managing the family business and the financial issues), the father’s attempt to connect those stressors with undertaking the offending behaviour, was a further weak excuse.

CONCLUSION OF THE RISK ISSUE

On all the evidence offered to the Court, in this case, I find:
(a) I am not satisfied that there is a connection with the father accessing CEM and real likelihood of then engaging in sexual abuse of the child X;

(b) I share the concerns expressed by Ms Q, Dr O and even Dr G, that the father’s inability to acknowledge a creditable reason for his offending behaviour, does allow the Court to find he presents as a possible emotional risk to X;

(c) the father’s failure to engage in the sort of treatment recommended, but rather just maintain long term therapy with Dr G (really for anxiety, depression and related relationship issues), is frankly unexplained. I am at loss to understand why he did not do so – but have formed the view that remaining with Dr G (a therapist not motivated to challenge the father’s underlying motivations to collect the CEM), was comfortable and helped him in other ways;

(d) I generally accept the evidence of Dr O and Ms Q – and I reject the contentions of the father’s Counsel that no weight should be attached to the evidence of Ms Q; and

(e) Counsel for the mother confirmed that the mother was not running a Russell & Close [1993] FamCA 62 type argument, however she submitted, and I accept, that the evidence of the father has raised a reasonable basis for concern in the mother’s mind, and as the uncontested primary carer, these concerns cannot be ignored.

In summary, my assessment of the father in the witness box was not of a dishonest person. He was trying to come to grips with why he offended, not only for himself, but I believe importantly to try and explain has actions to his extended family, who love and support him. His inability to genuinely do so has inhibited his capacity to demonstrate he is not a risk to X, and whether a differently targeted therapy from an expert psychiatrist or forensic psychologist would have opened an avenue of therapy not capable of exploration now, we simply do not know.
In short, the Court is bound, in the best interests of X, to assess whether other factors, as next discussed, outweigh the concerns of future risk which the Court finds, currently exist.

THE OTHER RELEVANT EVIDENCE

With so much attention in the trial directed to the issues of the father’s criminal offences and the assessment of risk, it is possible in these Reasons to make findings about other matters that arise for consideration within the matrix of the relevant primary and additional considerations prescribed by s 60CC(2) and (3) of the Act.
I rely upon, but do not repeat, findings already made earlier in these Reasons, as I now summarise in a narrative style the following findings necessary:
(a) The mother says that the father cannot add any value at all to the life and future development of X – not only because he has spent no time with her for some years – but because of the risks she asserts. This submission is not accepted. I find that the child would, over time, benefit from a safe relationship with her biological father – but that this benefit is not the only consideration, and as s 60CC(2)(c) makes clear, s 60CC(2)(b) must be given greater weight;

(b) For completeness, I do not find the mother or her household present any risks to the child. Although the father’s concession that it is in the best interests of X to live with her mother (and for her to have sole parental responsibility for major long-term decisions), supports my finding, I make it anyway. At some parts of the father’s evidence, he is critical of the mother, particularly her alleged “inconsistency” with initially supporting X spending time with him, before the Department’s intervention and subsequent bail conditions. I do not accept those criticisms, which I regard as easily explained by the mother’s attempts to try and balance her initial concerns with X’s positive experiences of spending time with the father. I have no doubt that the Department’s intervention and robust opinion and the influence of the Police Prosecution all combined to harden the mother’s attitude to the benefits of X spending time with her father. In this regard, where no Department officer was called to explain the basis for their assessment, I do accept the criticism raised by the father’s Counsel that, in some ways, the Department’s opinion did shape the progress of the matter. However, where this Court, after hearing all the evidence is best placed to decide what is in X’s best interests, I do not feel bound in any way by the initial assessment made by the Department – which was understandably cautious and was not shaped by the extensive evidence this Court has heard;

(c) The family report writer, Ms Q, captured some views expressed by the child in her interview with X in November 2021. She had last spent some supervised time with her father in March 2021. Ms Q decided not to facilitate the child spending time with the father on that day because she did not view it “as appropriate” to introduce X to her father again, unless a decision is made that time will resume between them (paragraph 82). In this limited context, which included one formal parent-child observation with X and her immediate household members, Ms Q records that:

(i) the child presented as a talkative and friendly child;

(ii) X drew a picture of her family and referred to her step-father as “daddy”. She identified her siblings as her half-brother C and step-sisters E and D; and

(iii) at paragraph 81, it is recorded that:

[X] told me she has “another dad”, however she does not remember his name. [X] advised that he “works away” and “I don’t see him”. She said that she would like to see her father again “cause I never get to see him”.
(d) The child now has a primary attachment to her mother, and a strong bond has formed with her stepfather Mr B, her sibling C and her “sisters”. Sadly, her memories of her biological father are limited, as are, I assess, her memories of the extended paternal family;

(e) The circumstances set out in the history, have created a situation where the mother (supported by her husband) have made decisions for X without seeking any input from the father;

(f) I have no concerns about the mother’s capacity or attitude to parenting. At best, the father’s capacity is untested and his attitude, in view of his criminal offending, is uncertain. Having said this, I do not doubt the father genuinely loves X and is desperate to form a relationship with her;

(g) Family violence issues do not loom large in this case;

(h) Because of the age of the child, and the many years of little or no contact between her and her father, it is highly speculative to assess the likelihood of future proceedings. If time is not to occur, then the father (who may take on board some of the concerns raised in this Judgment), might actually engage in appropriate therapy. The result of such therapy may, I put it no higher, lead to him feeling the threshold under s 65DAAA of the Act, can be exceeded in the future. If the child, well aware of her biological father’s existence, as she gets older expresses a view to meet her father – those wishes might need to be assessed at the time; and

(i) The Court, under s 60CC(3)(m), is required to consider any other relevant fact or circumstance. I have, with this factor in mind, considered the effect of a “no time” order on the extended paternal family – many of whom offered evidence to the Court. It is abundantly clear that they wish to have a relationship with X – who they all genuinely love and care about. The father’s actions have brought their relationship with X, as grandparents, aunts and uncles and of course cousins, to an end, and they show a sense of loss and grief. However, as brutal as that may be, none of the extended family sought to be parties to these proceedings or have sought specific orders made for them to spend time. My guess is that they were in a very difficult position of wanting to be seen to genuinely support the father and not diminish his chances of obtaining the orders he sought. They would also be mindful of the father’s emotional vulnerability and the likely damaging effect on him if an order for no time with X was made. They will need to offer him support after these orders are published.

CONCLUSION

Ms Q expressed that this is a complex case. It does not only involve the issue of whether the father poses a risk to the child, but also “if there is a benefit for [X] in re-establishing a relationship with her father”.
Above, I have comprehensively assessed the issue of risk to X. I rely on those findings, and repeat I am not satisfied X is at risk of being sexually abused by the father, applying the appropriate standard of proof, acknowledging s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth).
However, the father’s conduct, still unsatisfactorily explained, coupled with his delay in showing empathy and insight into the effect of his offending behaviour on X and the mother, remain serious and “untreated” concerns. I give him credit for supporting the mother’s application to change the child’s surname.
The mother’s case and her cross-examination did not establish she has sought to denigrate or demonise the father to X – rather she has just “moved on”, establishing a supportive adult relationship and a happy family unit now expanded by the birth of another child. I can understand her apprehension that reintroducing the father into X’s life now offers few benefits.
It is true that history cannot be changed – X as a result will always have a biological father, who she knows exists. I believe she has a right to understand the reality of her history, and as she gets older, she may (though curiosity or otherwise) wish to engage with her father and even the extended family. Her awareness of having a “biological” father found a need to allow him to send cards and gifts, as Ms Q suggested.
The mother’s behaviour to date, considering the circumstances she was presented with, reveals a highly child-focussed parent who has shown, and I am satisfied, will always strive to make decisions in the best interest of X. This is the ultimate protection for every child – tested when children become teenagers and often challenged when a child reaches an age of more independent thoughts and mature reflection. X is indeed lucky to have a mother of the quality she has to guide her development.
In these circumstances, but with some regret (including for delay in delivering these Reasons in this difficult case), I am persuaded by the ICL’s submissions, supported broadly by the mother, that it is not in X’s best interests to order she commence a period of re-introduction of time, in the uncertain hope, that it will inevitably lead to the father (and through him, his family), developing a meaningful relationship with X. The overall risks of commencing such a process now and the fact this could impact on her happy and settled life, in my assessment, outweigh the benefits of the process potentially being successful.
For these reasons, the Orders at the commencement of these Reasons, broadly those proposed by the ICL, are in the best interests of X as this time.
